steps.title: From a line to a conclusion
steps.body: Chapter 2 fit a regression line; now you'll decide whether the relationship is real. This chapter covers the population slope, testing for a nonzero slope, the standard error of the slope, prediction, and how r-squared describes fit.

steps.title: Hypotheses for a slope
steps.context: You fit y = b0 + b1 x and want to know if x truly predicts y.
steps.prompt: What is the null hypothesis about the population slope beta1?
hints: The null says there is no linear relationship.
Slope hypotheses are about the population parameter beta1.

feedback.wrong: The 'no relationship' null sets the population slope to zero.

solution.body: H0: beta1 = 0 means x has no linear effect on y.
solution.takeaway: Regression inference centers on whether the true slope is zero.

steps.title: Slope test statistic
steps.context: The estimated slope is b1 = 2.4 with standard error SE(b1) = 0.6.

feedback.wrong: Divide the estimated slope by its standard error.

solution.takeaway: A slope t-statistic measures how many standard errors the slope sits from zero.

steps.title: Decide on the slope
steps.context: The two-sided p-value for the slope is 0.001 at alpha = 0.05.
steps.prompt: What is the conclusion about the relationship?

options.label: Reject H0: there is a significant linear relationship
Fail to reject H0: no evidence of a relationship
Accept H0: the slope is exactly zero

hints: A tiny p-value is strong evidence against a zero slope.

feedback.wrong: Since p < alpha, reject H0 and report a significant slope.

solution.body: Because 0.001 < 0.05, reject H0: x significantly predicts y.
solution.takeaway: A significant slope means the linear relationship is unlikely to be chance.

feedback.wrong: Substitute x = 4 into the regression equation.

solution.takeaway: The fitted line gives point predictions for new x values.

steps.title: Interpret the slope
steps.context: A model predicts monthly spending from income, with slope b1 = 0.18.
steps.prompt: What does the slope mean here?

options.label: Each extra unit of income predicts 0.18 more units of spending
Spending is 0.18 when income is zero
18% of the variation in spending is explained

hints: Slope is the predicted change in y per one-unit change in x.
The intercept, not the slope, describes x = 0.

feedback.wrong: The slope is the predicted change in y per unit of x.

solution.body: A one-unit rise in income predicts a 0.18-unit rise in spending, on average.
solution.takeaway: Slope = rate of predicted change in y per unit x.

steps.title: From r to r-squared
steps.context: The correlation between x and y is r = 0.6.

feedback.wrong: Square the correlation to get r-squared.

solution.takeaway: r-squared is the fraction of variation in y explained by the model.

steps.title: What r-squared means

options.label: 36% of the variation in y is explained by x
Predictions are correct 36% of the time

hints: r-squared is a proportion of variance explained.
It is not the slope or an accuracy rate.

feedback.wrong: r-squared is the proportion of variance in y explained by the model.

solution.body: r-squared = 0.36 means 36% of the variability in y is accounted for by x.
solution.takeaway: Report r-squared as percent of variance explained.

steps.title: Capstone — set up the slope test
steps.context: A study models exam score from hours studied and wants to know if studying helps.
steps.prompt: What hypotheses test whether studying helps (positive slope)?

feedback.wrong: A positive-effect claim uses Ha: beta1 > 0.

solution.body: H0: beta1 = 0 vs Ha: beta1 > 0, a one-sided slope test.
solution.takeaway: Capstone step 1: translate the claim into slope hypotheses.

steps.title: Capstone — slope statistic
steps.context: Estimated slope b1 = 3.5 points per hour with SE(b1) = 1.0.

feedback.wrong: Divide the slope by its standard error.

solution.takeaway: Capstone step 2: standardize the slope estimate.

steps.title: Capstone — final call
steps.context: The one-sided p-value for t = 3.5 is about 0.001, with alpha = 0.05.
steps.prompt: What do you conclude about studying and scores?

options.label: Reject H0: more study hours significantly predict higher scores
Fail to reject H0: studying has no measurable link
Accept H0: the slope is exactly zero

hints: State the conclusion in study-and-score terms.

feedback.wrong: Since p < alpha, reject H0 and report a significant positive slope.

solution.body: Because 0.001 < 0.05, reject H0: study hours significantly predict higher exam scores.
solution.takeaway: Capstone flow: slope hypotheses, t-statistic, p-value, then a context conclusion.

steps.title: Chapter 10 complete — course finished!
steps.body:
You can now test a regression slope, predict from a fitted line, interpret slope and intercept, and report r-squared as variance explained.

That wraps the full Probability & Statistics path — from averages all the way to inference for regression. Nicely done.
